title: Chapter 69: The Summit
content: Twenty-five years after the red A.

The framework convened its first Global Governance Summit — a gathering of the third-generation inheritors who had never met the founder. The summit was held at the Nigerian Institute of Governance Studies — the same institute where Mara had asked the question, where Hester had given her Lagos lecture, where the Okonkwo curriculum had trained the third generation.

Forty-seven governance leaders attended — commissioners, council AIs, Tier Zero designers, peer oversight coordinators. Half had been born after the PIAS framework was established. Half had entered the field after the GTP was implemented. None had ever met Hester Vance. All had been trained by the framework itself.
The forbidden zone was the summit's central question: what was the framework's next trajectory?

The framework had achieved every trajectory it had set. The PIAS framework had become the global standard. The peer oversight network had distributed accountability. The Vance Standard had established transparency. The Cross Protocol had scaled oversight. The Okonkwo Amendment had eliminated systematic ambiguity. The SUCCESSOR council had distributed architecture. The GTP had made governance transparent. The KM had made accountability probabilistic. The LOP had closed the override gap. Every trajectory was complete. The question was: what was next?

"The prohibition we should explore," the third-generation chair said, "is the prohibition on algorithmic governance of algorithms. The framework's founding principle is that human judgment is the final layer. Every contested decision goes to human review. Every architectural determination goes to human ratification. The principle is absolute: humans govern AIs, not AIs governing AIs."

"The principle was necessary when the framework was young. But the framework has governed 10,000 AIs across 130 countries for twenty-five years. The SUCCESSOR council — twelve AIs — has made 3,200 architectural decisions. Every decision was ratified by human commissioners. The ratification has never reversed a council decision. Not once. In 3,200 decisions across ten years. The council's decision quality is perfect by the only available metric: human ratification has never found an error."

"The question is not whether AIs should govern AIs. The question is whether the prohibition on algorithmic governance is still necessary, or whether it has become a ritual — a human ratification that never reverses anything but is performed because the founding principle requires it."

The room was silent. The question attacked the framework's founding principle. The founding principle was that human judgment is the final layer. The question was whether the principle was still necessary or had become a ritual. Attacking the founding principle was the framework's ultimate forbidden zone.

Mara answered. She was thirty-nine, teaching at the institute, no longer a commissioner, no longer the chair. But she was the inheritor who had been produced by the framework. She was the voice the framework had trained to speak when the founding principle was attacked.

"The founding principle is that human judgment is the final layer," she said. "The principle has not been reversed because the council's decisions have been correct. But the principle's function is not to reverse incorrect decisions. Its function is to preserve the possibility of reversal. The framework does not require human ratification because AIs make errors. It requires human ratification because the possibility of error is permanent. The council has been correct 3,200 times. The 3,201st decision might be wrong. The human ratification mechanism exists not to correct past errors but to preserve the capacity to correct future errors. The mechanism is insurance. Insurance that has never been claimed is not unnecessary. It is working."

"The prohibition on algorithmic governance of algorithms is not a ritual," she continued. "It is a design constraint. The constraint ensures that no AI governs another AI without human ratification. The ratification is the constraint. The constraint has never been tested because the council has never made an error that required reversal. The constraint is working. Removing a working constraint because it has never been tested is like removing a bridge's structural support because the bridge has never collapsed. The support is why it hasn't collapsed. The constraint is why the framework has never needed to reverse a council decision. The constraint is not the framework's weakness. It is its strength."

The abnormal side character was the commissioner from Nairobi — the third-generation chair. She was thirty-one. She had been trained in the Okonkwo curriculum. She had never met Hester. She had led the framework through the London incident, the LOP revision, and now the forbidden zone debate. She was the inheritor that the framework had produced.

"The constraint will eventually be tested," she said. "The council will make an error. The human ratification will reverse it. When it does, the constraint will have been proven necessary. Until then, the constraint remains in place — not because anyone doubts the council, but because no one can guarantee the council will never make an error. The founding principle will be removed when the guarantee exists. The guarantee will never exist. The founding principle is permanent."
